content.problemStatement:
Now, we will discuss how to find the prime factorization of any positive integer $n$ in $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$ Time Complexity.

The main observation is that $n$ cannot have more than one prime factor which is > $\sqrt{n}$.\
Why is this?\
Suppose there were two distinct prime factors $p$ and $q$ of $n$ which are both > $\sqrt{n}$. Then, $p * q$ would be $> n$, which is a contradiction. Thus, we have proved our observation.

Our new algorithm is very similar to the previous one, but we will now only run the for loop till $sqrt(n)$ instead of till $n$. And we might end up with a prime number at the end (which is > $\sqrt{n}$ ), which should also be considered.

Like in the previous algo, the number is repeatedly divided by each prime factor until no further division is feasible.\
Towards the conclusion of the algorithm, if a remainder greater than 1 persists, it signifies that the remaining value itself is a prime factor. Consequently, this remaining value is incorporated into the list of identified factors. 


**Pseudo Code**:
```
Function factor(int n)
	Take an empty array called ret.
	for (int i = 2; i * i  1) 
                Add n to the end of ret
	return ret
```

This algorithm runs in 
$\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n})$
 time, because the for loop checks
divisibility for at most 
$\sqrt{n}$
 values. Even though there is a while loop
inside the for loop, dividing 
$n$
 by 
$i$
 quickly reduces the value of 
$n$
, which
means that the outer for loop runs less iterations, which actually speeds up the
code.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
